children.text: Latinizing Reggaetón

By the early 2000s, reggaetón’s rapid rise—punctuated by the crossover success of "Gasolina" and other Luny Tunes compositions—positioned the genre for incorporation into U.S. and global media markets. This moment coincided with the so-called “Latin Boom,” in which artists like Ricky Martin, Enrique Iglesias, and Shakira were already establishing the commercial viability of “Latin” music for Anglophone audiences. Within this framework, reggaetón was rebranded as “Latin" hip-hop, a move that expanded its reach while initiating a process of discursive flattening that erased the Black diasporic and transnational origins of the genre.

This process of Latinization was not neutral. It operated through what can be understood as a set of racialized and aesthetic “safety frames” that rendered reggaetón legible and profitable to global audiences. These frames privileged artists who conformed more closely to a dominant “Latin look” (light complexion, European features, racially ambiguous presentation) while downplaying or erasing the genre’s Afro-Caribbean and Black diasporic origins. Simultaneously, the raciolinguistic enregisterment of Spanish as the defining marker of Latinidad allowed the genre’s linguistic surface to stand in for cultural authenticity, even as the Blackness that originally animated its sonic and performative practices was increasingly backgrounded. In this sense, reggaetón’s crossover depended not only on translation across markets, but on a partial detachment from the Blackness that had initially rendered it politically and culturally potent.

content.caption: From Solis Miranda (2022) Shifts in Reggaetón Demographics after Mainstream Commercialization (Top L-R: El General, Tego Calderón, Don Omar / Bottom L-R: J Balvin, Maluma, Bad Bunny)

children.text: As reggaetón became a dominant force in global popular music, these dynamics intensified. The genre’s sonic templates for embodied performance rooted in Black-diasporic traditions remained central to its appeal, yet their circulation was increasingly mediated by artists and industry structures positioned closer to whiteness, economic privilege, and transnational mobility. The result is a political economy in which Afro-, Afro-Caribbean, and Caribbean expressive forms more broadly are widely consumed as markers of a generalized, non-threatening
urban

Latinidad, while the communities and identities most directly tied to their emergence are rendered less visible within mainstream representations. Reggaetón’s Latinization, then, is not simply a story of expansion, but of transformation: a reconfiguration of the genre’s indexical field in which Blackness, language, and marketability are continuously negotiated.
Below are some of the most pivotal moments of reggaetón's continued transformation and globalization over the past two decades.
"Danza Kuduro" and Zumba

“Danza Kuduro” marks one of reggaetón’s clearest early-2010s crossings into global dance-pop. Released by Don Omar and Lucenzo in 2010, the song fused reggaetón’s Puerto Rican urbano framework with kuduro, an Angolan-derived dance music that had already circulated through Lusophone African and Portuguese-speaking diasporic networks. Its success reframed reggaetón not only as club music or urban youth expression, but as a participatory global dance template as something that could be learned, repeated, choreographed, and embodied across linguistic and national boundaries.

This was amplified by the rise of Zumba, which moved Latin urban rhythms into gyms, dance studios, cruise ships, and community centers. Through Zumba, reggaetón’s dembow-derived rhythmic sensibility became newly legible as “Latin dance” for global audiences, often detached from the genre’s underground history and racial politics. In this moment, reggaetón entered a new circuit of circulation: not only radio and clubs, but choreographed wellness economies where Afro-diasporic rhythmic templates were repackaged as accessible, high-energy, multicultural fun.
"Bailando"

“Bailando” by Enrique Iglesias featuring Cuban artists Descemer Bueno and the duo Gente de Zona represents a key crossover moment in which reggaetón-adjacent rhythmic and vocal aesthetics were absorbed into a polished Latin pop framework. The track draws from an expanding urbano-pop soundscape in which dembow, Caribbean percussion, flamenco guitar, and Cuban dance music coexist, helping normalize the presence of reggaetón’s rhythmic vocabulary within mainstream Spanish-language pop.

Its global success demonstrated that reggaetón no longer needed to be framed as underground, illicit, or oppositional to circulate widely. Instead, “Bailando” presented a smoother, more romanticized and mainstream-friendly iteration of the genre’s sonic and visual language, foregrounding dance, intimacy, and pan-Latin appeal. In doing so, it helped prepare the conditions for the late-2010s urbano boom by showcasing how reggaetón could function as a central  and highly profitable infrastructure for Latin pop.
Medellín in the Mix

By the mid-2010s, Medellín emerged as a major center of reggaetón production, shifting the genre’s geography beyond Puerto Rico, Panama, and New York. Artists such as J Balvin, Maluma, and Karol G helped consolidate a Colombian urbano sound that was more explicitly pop-facing than earlier underground iterations. Their rise redistributed the genre’s authority, positioning Medellín as a site where reggaetón could be commercialized and exported globally.

Each of these artists showcased the artistic range of reggaetón-based pop music in innovative ways. J Balvin framed reggaetón as cosmopolitan, Maluma pushed the genre further into romantic pop and celebrity culture, and Karol G expanded the visibility and sonic range of women within the urban market with unapologetic performances of agentive femininity.  Together, they demonstrate how reggaetón’s templates could be revoiced through Colombian aesthetics and industry infrastructures. Medellín’s emergence thus marks a key moment in the genre’s transnationalization, where reggaetón becomes less tied to a single national origin and more recognizable as a flexible, global pop language.
Latin Trap

Around the same time as the popification and Colombianification of reggaetón, a new countercultural subgenre of reggaetón began to emerge as a countercurrent to the increasingly polished, Latinized reggaetón circulating the global pop markets. Rooted in the sonic aesthetics of U.S. trap (e.g., slower tempos, heavy bass, emphasis on rap flow), Puerto Rican artists began to reactivate the underground sensibilities stripped from reggaetón during its crossover. Artist such as Anuel AA, Bryant Myers, Ozuna, and Bad Bunny used trap to foreground street-coded and localized authenticity amidst an increasingly globalized and culturally flattened industry. These productions helped re-center Puerto Rico within the urbano landscape while also expanding what urbano could sound and feel like by restoring rawness and lyrical confrontation to the market. Moreover, these tracks opened the doors for more fluid experimentation between trap, reggaetón, and broader Caribbean/Latin musical templates. While Latin Trap was met with similar cultural critiques as the Underground decades earlier, technological advancements in music streaming/distribution catapulted Trap to mainstream popularity at speeds that significantly outpaced its predecessor.
The "Despacito" Effect

The first half of the 2010s was scored by a mixture of the Latinized, pop-infused mainstream reggaetón tracks produced by the Puerto Rican vocalists who pioneered the genre's underground origins, the pop-facing, Medellín-based iteration quickly gaining traction, and the Trap productions carving out new spaces and models of what reggaetón could do and sound like. This period saw the release of some of the decade's most popular songs, including "Hasta el Amancer" by Nicky Jam (which included an English translation), "Criminal" by Ozuna and Dominican pop artist Natti Natasha, "Mayores" by Mexican-American singer Becky G and Bad Bunny, and "Felices los 4" by Maluma. By 2017, the ranks of reggaetón hitmakers had drastically shifted in terms of sound and demographics.  At this point, "reggaetón" and "urbano" had become synonymous, particularly amongst newer fans, for denoting a musical category ranging from pop music with the dembow inflection to trp songs with artists known to
also
sing reggaetón.

It was in this era of blurred genre lines that "Despacito" was released. This song—performed by Puerto Rican balladist Luis Fonsi and Daddy Yankee—marks a decisive inflection point in reggaetón's global consolidation. Since its release, the song has broken over seven World Records, including:
Most Liked Video Online
" (acquiring over 29 million likes as of August, 2018)
Most-Streamed Song Worldwide
" with 4.6 billion streams as of July, 2017
Most Weeks at No. 1
" on the Hot Latin Songs chart, spending over 56 weeks at #1
Most Viewed YouTube Video
",  a record which it broke itself again after becoming the first video to hit 3, 4, 5, and 6 billion views.

The song—which has gained over 9 Billion views as of April 2026—demonstrated that a Spanish-language track grounded in reggaetón’s rhythmic sensibilities and general Caribbean social semiotics (contextualized in San Juan's La Perla Neighborhood) could dominate global pop charts without translation. Its viral spread, further amplified by the remix featuring Justin Bieber, completely shattered industry assumptions about the limits of non-English music.

Crucially, “Despacito” presented a polished, melodic, and romanticized iteration of reggaetón forged in the musical and artistic melting pot that scored the Industry at the time. That is, while the rhythmic structure draws from the dembow and Daddy Yankee does rap over this riddim, the instrumentation foregrounds an acoustic guitar, a pop vocal delivery, and sensual yet widely palatable imagery, both in terms of the lyrics and the visuals of the music video. Moreover, Despacito sent a shockwave through the mainstream Latin Urban industry by cementing the absolute profitability and consumability of products that look, feel, and sound "Caribbean" (in the most general sense). The effects of this, in terms of how mainstream reggaetón is produced for global consumption, are still palpable today. Moreover, the success of the song intensified tensions around topics of language, race, and representation as fans, critics, and creatives began to raise questions about which sounds, cultural artifacts, and identities are most easily globalized, and who actually benefits from such commodifications. This was especially palpable in debates regarding the necessity of the Justin Bieber remix given the success of the original, an overall widespread displeasure with Bieber's accented delivery of the chorus and Fonsi's inclusion of an English verse, and the rise of "covers" by White, Anglophone US Americans in particular delivering the lyrics in "Mock Spanish".
¿Dónde está Mi Gente?

"Despacito" ushered in a new wave of popularity for Latin Urban music. “Mi Gente” by J Balvin and French DJ Willy William represents a parallel trajectory, foregrounding rhythm and collective participation over melodic crossover. Blending reggaetón with electronic and global club production, the track operates through repetition, chant, and embodied engagement rather than narrative or lyrical complexity.

Its minimal, chant-like structure made it highly scalable across linguistic boundaries, circulating through dance challenges, DJ culture, and social media virality. The remix featuring Beyoncé further amplified its reach while preserving its Spanish-language core, signaling a shift in which global pop artists increasingly adapt to reggaetón’s sonic and linguistic frameworks. That is, “Mi Gente” demonstrated that reggaetón’s global success does not depend solely on pop softening or romantic framing. Instead, it affirms the power of rhythmic intensity, repetition, and collective address, positioning reggaetón as a shared, transnational dance language grounded in Afro-diasporic and Caribbean musical traditions.
Super Bowl LIV

The Super Bowl LIV Halftime Show headlined by Shakira and Jennifer Lopez stands as a landmark moment for reggaetón and Latin music’s visibility within the U.S. mainstream, even though the performance itself was not centered on reggaetón as a genre. Framed primarily through pop, dance, and Latin crossover hits, the show nevertheless strategically incorporated urbano through guest appearances by Bad Bunny and J Balvin who performed tracks like "Mi Gente" and "I Like It," alongside reggaetón-inflected moments in Shakira and J-Lo's own catalogs (e.g., a Salsa remix of Shakira's reggaetón hit "Chantaje" with Maluma, a dancebreak set to JLo's reggaetón song "El Anillo" remixed with Dominican artist NFasis' hit "Lento").

In doing so, the halftime show positioned reggaetón not as a marginal or niche genre, but as an essential component of contemporary Latin popular music, worthy of inclusion on one of the most visible entertainment stages in the world. This moment signals a broader shift in the cultural economy: reggaetón’s sounds, artists, and aesthetics had become sufficiently normalized—and profitable—to be showcased within a highly curated, national spectacle, even if still embedded within a broader, more palatable Latin pop framework.
"Bichota" and On Sounding Caribbean

Reggaetón came into the 2020s with more demand and global popularity than had been historically thought impossible for non-English language musics. The 2020s only continued to build on these foundations, particularly the words released by Bad Bunny and Karol G. In fact, “Bichota” by Karol G marks a pivotal moment in the consolidation of reggaetón’s global economy because it makes undeniably explicit what earlier successes like “Despacito” and “Mi Gente” had already begun to capitalize and strategize: Caribbeanness is not a liability in global markets—it is profitable.

While Karol G had previously operated within the smoother, pop-oriented Medellín urbano aesthetic, “Bichota” represents a deliberate stylistic and linguistic shift toward a more overtly Caribbeanized reggaetón performance. Most notably, this includes her adoption of phonological features associated with Puerto Rican Spanish—features that carry strong indexical ties to reggaetón’s historical centers of production and its Afro-Caribbean roots. This shift is not incidental. In the wake of “Despacito,” which proved that Spanish-language reggaetón could dominate global charts, and “Mi Gente,” which demonstrated the scalability of rhythm and collective address without linguistic accommodation, “Bichota” moves one step further: it foregrounds the value of
sounding Caribbean

. The track’s performance does not dilute or neutralize these features for broader accessibility; instead, it amplifies them as stylistic capital. In doing so, Karol G aligns herself more directly with the genre’s perceived locus of authenticity, signaling that proximity to Caribbean linguistic and sonic norms enhances—not limits—global appeal.

At the same time, this moment reveals the continued asymmetries within reggaetón’s political economy. The ability to strategically adopt Caribbean features as a resource underscores how Caribbeanness circulates as a commodifiable aesthetic, one that can be mobilized by artists positioned within different national and racial formations. “Bichota” thus encapsulates a key transformation in the genre’s indexical field: where earlier industry logics often required a distancing from Blackness and Afro-Caribbean specificity to achieve crossover success, the late 2010s demonstrate that these very features can be revalued as desirable markers of authenticity, edge, and marketability.
Bad Bunny

Benito Antonio Martínez Ocasio—known professionally as Bad Bunny—is the most successful artist ever to grace the reggaetón stage. His visibility and influence cannot be overstated. Bad Bunny first emerged in 2016 as part of the growing Trap market, and as he has become integrated into the mainstream, he has continuously re-centered Puerto Rico within an increasingly global market, not by rejecting commercialization, but by reshaping its terms. His 2020 album "YHLQMDLG" ("
yo hago lo que me da la gana

", meaning "I do what I want"), operates as both homage and intervention. Sonically, it draws heavily from early-2000s reggaetón, reactivating dembow-driven minimalism, DJ-centric sequencing, and underground textures. Yet semiotically, it reasserts Puerto Rico as a primary stylistic resource rather than something to be flattened for crossover or to appeal for an imaginatively homogenous "Latin" audience.

This intervention becomes especially visible in “Yo perreo sola,” a track that simultaneously returns to the sonic template of classic perreo and radically reconfigures its gender politics. By performing in drag and centering the autonomy of women within the dance space, Bad Bunny disrupts reggaetón’s historically masculinist performance norms while preserving the embodied, rhythmic intensity that defines the genre. This move came at a particularly salient moment in Puerto Rico's broader cultural debates surrounding gender and sexuality norms, as his choice to perform in drag and to include broader feminist messaging—including the motto "
ni una menos

" (lit. not even one less)—was widely received as a response to the killing of Alexa Negrón Luciano, a homeless trans youth. In doing so, Bad Bunny showcased how the musical contours of reggaetón could remain intact, all the while reclaiming reggaetón as a site of cultural critique and political discourse and challenging ideologies about the performance of gender and agency in reggaetón.

By the end of 2020, Bad Bunny had risen to superstar status in Spanish-language music markets. In 2022, he expanded his project of using reggateón to recenter Puerto Rico by releasing "Un Verano Sin Ti", an album that scales Caribbeanness itself as a global, organizing aesthetic. The album moves fluidly across reggaetón, dembow, mambo, reggae, house, and indie pop, but remains anchored in a distinctly Caribbean sensibility—sonically, linguistically, and affectively. Rather than presenting reggaetón as a single genre, it positions the Caribbean as a dynamic, internally diverse soundscape whose musical forms can circulate globally without losing their regional grounding. This is not crossover in the earlier sense of adaptation to Anglo pop norms; it is a re-centering of Caribbean expressive culture as the norm to which global audiences attune.

Recent work such as “DTMF” continues this trajectory, coming to break Despacito's record for the longest run at No. 1 of the Hot Latin Songs chart in April of 2026. This song, album, tour, and all accompanying materials have further consolidated the idea that Puerto Rican urbano can function simultaneously as local expression and global infrastructure. Across these projects, Bad Bunny demonstrates that reggaetón’s commercial dominance need not rely on the erasure or sanitization of its roots. Instead, his work shows that linguistic specificity, rhythmic intensity, and Caribbean cultural references can themselves drive mass appeal. In this sense, Bad Bunny does not simply participate in reggaetón’s globalization—he redefines its terms, establishing Caribbeanness, rather than its dilution, as the genre’s most powerful and profitable organizing principle.
Benito Bowl & Beyond
The most recent instance of global reggaetón achievements has been
Benito Bowl

, a colloquial renaming of the Super Bowl LX Halftime Show headlined by Bad Bunny. This marks a historic rupture in the cultural and linguistic politics of global popular music. On February 8, 2026, Bad Bunny became the first artist to headline the Super Bowl halftime show with a performance delivered primarily, and effectively entirely, in Spanish. This was not a moment of translation or accommodation; it was a full-scale reorientation of the stage itself. The most-watched entertainment platform in the United States was reorganized around Puerto Rican Spanish, Caribbean sound, and Afro-diasporic musical forms, with no concessions made to Anglophone expectations.

What makes the “Benito Bowl” especially significant is how the performance—framed as a celebration of Puerto Rican culture, complete with traditional instruments, local iconography, a reggaetón-heavy setlist, and a guest appearance by Ricky Martin—positioned Caribbean expressive culture not as an exotic supplement to American spectacle, but as its organizing center. Moreover, the performance masterfully weaves political critiques of U.S. imperalism and racist models of national belonging into its sets and organization. Even the audience logic shifts here: Bad Bunny not only told the global audience they had "four months" to learn Spanish upon announcing his headlining spot, he also explicitly reframed participation around feeling and movement while including moments of double voicing specifically for Latino/Puerto Rican listeners and viewers that would remain otherwise inaccessible to the global Other.  In this sense, the “Benito Bowl” is not just representation—it is infrastructural power. It demonstrates that reggaetón and Puerto Rican popular culture have reached a point where they can dictate the terms of global visibility, rather than adapt to them. The halftime show becomes less a site where Latin music is included and more a site where U.S. mainstream culture temporarily reorganizes itself around Caribbean norms. What was once underground, racialized, and censored now commands the most surveilled stage in American media, not by erasing its linguistic or cultural specificity, but by amplifying it.
